topic: Parsing - Writing a JSON Parser from Scratch

turns.content: Hey, I know you're the go-to person for this. I've read about writing a JSON parser from scratch using recursive descent but it's still fuzzy in my head. How do I actually start building one in Rust without relying on serde_json?

Welcome aboard! Don't worry, the first step is just setting up a simple tokenizer that breaks the input string into tokens like strings, numbers, and operators. In Rust, you'll likely use a String or Vec<char> to hold your token stream, then write separate recursive descent functions for each JSON structure type like objects, arrays, and primitive values.
Okay, so I need a tokenizer first. What exactly does that function look like? Does it return something specific?

Your tokenizer should iterate over the input bytes or characters and yield tokens as they appear. A good Rust type for this might be an enum with variants like Token::String, Token::Number, Token::{open_brace}, etc., so your recursive descent functions can pattern match on these tokens instead of parsing raw characters directly.
Got it. So I'm building a custom enum for tokens and then calling the tokenizer once to get that stream? And how do I handle Unicode escapes like \u0041 without using serde_json?

Exactly, you'll call your tokenizer function on the full input string first. For Unicode escapes, add a special case in your tokenizer that detects the backslash-u pattern and converts those hex characters into actual UTF-8 code points using Rust's char::from_u32 or std::str::parse_utf8 methods before yielding the resulting character token.
I see. Now I need to write the recursive descent parser itself. What's the best way to structure this in Rust to avoid stack overflows or other common mistakes?

Keep your main parse function as a single entry point that calls helper functions for objects, arrays, and primitives. You can define each parser as a separate free function or a trait implementation if you want to reuse logic across different JSON formats. Using an enum for tokens makes pattern matching cleaner than raw string comparisons.
One last thing: how do I track error positions so that when there's a syntax error, the user gets a helpful message like 'line 5, column 12' instead of just 'parse error'?

You'll maintain a Position struct with line and column fields updated as your tokenizer advances through the input. Each time you encounter an error in your recursive descent functions, capture that position into your Result or custom Error type so it can be formatted into human-readable messages later.
